passages.explanation: When they draw near to Jerusalem and come to Bethphage at the Mount of Olives, Jesus sends two disciples: go into the village, and you'll find a donkey tied with her colt. Untie them and bring them to me. If anyone says anything, tell them the Lord needs them. This fulfils Zechariah 9:9 — your King comes to you, humble, riding on a donkey, on a colt, the foal of a donkey. The disciples do as instructed. They place their garments on the donkey, and Jesus sits on them. The great multitude spreads their garments on the road. Others cut branches and spread them. The crowds cry out: Hosanna to the son of David! Blessed is he who comes in the name of the Lord! Hosanna in the highest! When he enters Jerusalem, the whole city is stirred: who is this? The crowds say: this is the prophet Jesus from Nazareth of Galilee. Jesus enters Jerusalem as a king — but on a donkey, not a warhorse. It's a deliberate claim of messiahship wrapped in humility.

Jesus enters the temple and drives out all who buy and sell there. He overturns the tables of the money changers and the seats of those selling doves. He tells them: it is written, my house shall be called a house of prayer, but you have made it a den of robbers! The blind and the lame come to him in the temple, and he heals them. When the chief priests and scribes see the wonderful things he does and the children crying out Hosanna to the son of David, they're indignant. Jesus responds: haven't you ever read — out of the mouths of infants and nursing babies you have perfected praise? He leaves and goes to Bethany to spend the night. Jesus clears the temple not because commerce existed — pilgrims needed services — but because exploitation had replaced worship. The temple had become a marketplace where the vulnerable were fleeced.

In the morning, returning to the city, Jesus is hungry. He sees a fig tree by the road and comes to it but finds nothing on it except leaves. He says: let there be no fruit from you from now on forever. The fig tree immediately withers. The disciples are amazed: how did the fig tree wither so quickly? Jesus answers: if you have faith and don't doubt, you'll not only do what was done to the fig tree, but even if you tell this mountain to be thrown into the sea, it will be done. All things you ask in prayer, believing, you will receive. The fig tree is a prophetic sign — like the temple, it has leaves (the appearance of life) but no fruit. Israel's religious infrastructure looks impressive but produces nothing.

Jesus comes into the temple, and the chief priests and elders confront him while he's teaching: by what authority do you do these things? Who gave you this authority? Jesus answers with a counter-question: the baptism of John — was it from heaven or from men? They deliberate among themselves: if we say from heaven, he'll ask why we didn't believe John. If we say from men, we fear the multitude, because they all consider John a prophet. They answer: we don't know. Jesus says: neither will I tell you by what authority I do these things. They won't answer honestly, so Jesus won't answer at all. Their question about authority is exposed as political calculation, not genuine inquiry.

What do you think? A man has two sons. He tells the first: go work in my vineyard today. The son says I will not — but afterwards he regrets it and goes. He tells the second the same. The son says I go, sir — but doesn't go. Which did the will of his father? They say: the first. Jesus tells them: most certainly I tell you, the tax collectors and prostitutes are entering the Kingdom of God before you. John came in the way of righteousness and you didn't believe him — but the tax collectors and prostitutes did. Even when you saw that, you still didn't repent and believe. The parable is blunt: religious profession without obedience is worse than initial refusal followed by repentance.

Hear another parable: a landowner plants a vineyard, sets a hedge, digs a wine press, builds a tower, leases it to farmers, and goes abroad. When harvest time comes, he sends servants to collect his fruit. The farmers beat one servant, kill another, and stone a third. He sends more servants — they treat them the same way. Finally he sends his son, thinking they'll respect him. But the farmers see the son and say: this is the heir — let's kill him and seize his inheritance. They grab him, throw him out of the vineyard, and kill him. When the owner comes, what will he do? They answer: he'll miserably destroy those miserable men and lease the vineyard to other farmers who will give him the fruit in its season. Jesus says: didn't you ever read — the stone which the builders rejected has become the cornerstone? The Kingdom of God will be taken away from you and given to a nation producing its fruit. The chief priests and Pharisees hear the parables and perceive he's speaking about them. They want to seize him but fear the multitudes, who consider him a prophet. The vineyard is Israel. The servants are the prophets. The son is Jesus. The parable is a compressed history of God's patience — and a warning that patience has a limit.
